metadata.description: An OUTCOME: a target host's logs are shipped to a central log sink, tagged with the host's identity. The consumer declares 'I need a LogShipper on host X' — nothing about HOW. A provider realizes it; the automation method (an Ansible role today via ansible-runner, an AAP job template tomorrow, a container, a script) is the provider's encapsulated internal mechanism and never appears in this type. Swapping the method is an internal provider change with zero impact on this contract or the consumer — that invisibility is the point. Worked realization: a provider's Ansible `alloy` role (journald -> Loki). See DCM architecture/integrations/automation-outcome-providers.md.

context.purpose: Declares the outcome that a host's logs reach the central sink — without saying anything about how.
context.plain_description: A statement of outcome: logs from a target host, shipped to a named sink URL, tagged with the host's identity. The consumer never sees the mechanism — whether a provider satisfies it with an automation-managed agent, a container, or something else is the provider's private business, and swapping mechanisms changes nothing in this record. Realized state reports whether shipping is healthy and when the last successful delivery happened, which drives staleness detection.
use_when: You need logs-from-host-X-land-in-the-central-store as a declared, checkable fact per host.
You need shipping health and last-delivery time surfaced for drift and staleness detection.
not_for: The log store itself — that is its own resource (e.g. a Software.Service running the sink); this type is the per-host shipping outcome.
Metrics or trace collection — not covered; this type is logs.
works_with: Compute.BareMetalHost / Compute.VirtualMachine — the target host whose logs are shipped.
Software.Service — the central log store the sink URL points at.

target.description: Whose logs. The host the shipper runs on / collects from. (In a fuller model this becomes a typed relationship to a host resource; for now it is a declarative identifier.)

sink.description: Where the logs go — the central log store endpoint. Vendor-neutral (a Loki/OTLP push URL); the store itself is another entity.

url.description: Log-store push endpoint (e.g. a Loki push URL).

source.description: Which logs to ship: the systemd journal (default) or on-disk files. An outcome property — not a method detail.

labels.description: Extra stream labels to attach beyond the inherent host/unit identity (e.g. environment, role). Optional.

status.description: Realized health of the shipper (e.g. running / degraded), as reported by discovery.

shipping_to.description: The sink URL the shipper is actually delivering to (echo of realized config).

last_shipped_at.description: Timestamp of the most recent successful delivery (RFC 3339) — drives drift / staleness detection.
